Wonderful. Now I understand exactly what you want.

That said, I do not think method chaining between `furl` objects and their URL
components (`Path`, `Query`, and `Fragment`) is common enough nor profound
enough to warrant additional API complexity. Furl's existing API, without
inter-URL-component method chaining, is clear enough and Pythonic enough.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=b')
>>> f.query.set({'b':'REPLACED})
>>> # Do something with the original furl object <f>...
```

That, and the addition of new parameters

```python
>>> f.set(query_set=...)
```

methods

```python
>>> f.query_set(...)
```

and/or attributes

```python
>>> f.query.set(...).parent.url
```

isn't worth the additional API foot print.

If many others want similar method chaining tree, I will reconsider this
issue. If you are one such other person, please feel free to re-open this issue.

In the mean time, if you absolutely, positively must method chain Query
modifications into `furl.set()`, you can provide `query=str(f.query.set(...))`
to `furl.set()`. Like

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=b')
>>> f.set(query=str(f.query.set({'b':'REPLACED'}))).url
'http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=REPLACED'
```

This is, of course, ugly and not recommended.

In conclusion, I hope my explanations and thoughts help. Thank you for bringing
this issue to my attention answering my questions, Kimi. If you have anything to
add to this issue or otherwise, don't hesitate to let me know.

While I'm not sure what the justification is for this behavior by default, it's very easily worked-around. It takes longer to post a complaint on Github than to look at the method signatures to find out how ot change the undesired behavior.

The constructors for `AttrMap` and `AttrDefault` take a `sequence_type` argument, which you just need to set to `list`:

```python
>>> m=AttrMap(sequence_type=list)
>>> m.l = [1, 2, 3]
>>> m.l
[1, 2, 3]
>>> type(m.l)
<class 'list'>
```
Strangely, `AttrDict` doesn't take this parameter, but it's possible to change its behavior as well with a hack:

```python
>>> d=AttrDict()
>>> d._setattr('_sequence_type', list)
>>> d.l = [1, 2, 3]
>>> d.l
[1, 2, 3]
>>> type(d.l)
<class 'list'>
```

I think I understand.
Lets see if we agree on this:
## The default case:
-  loop is not set thus `loop=None`..
- `ConnectionPool.loop = None`
- `connection = ConnectionPool.get_connection()`
- `connection.loop == ConnectionPool.loop == None`
- `connection` calls for example `asyncio.sleep(5, loop=self.loop)` -> `self.loop == None` 
- `asyncio.sleep` does `asyncio.get_event_loop()` loop is thread local and works as expected.

## New case
- `loop = event_loop`
- As above, but now connection is not thread safe anymore, because `event_loop` is not thread safe.
- I think we should assume that if someone explicitly defines a `loop`, they are expecting everything to work in one thread.
- Otherwise the user would not explicitly define the `loop` but would define a new event loop policy using the `async.set_event_loop_policy()` function.

I personally think that this way we have both situations covered.

Hi,
first of all sorry for my late reply. I’m absolutely fine with the supporting bmm only in the files section. I started with the tool section in analogy to .ucf files, but at the I found it better in the files section for the same reason as you. 
Regarding the bmm workflow:  Stefan already outined the purpose of BMM files. For this reason they are two variants of a bmm: The first  variant contains only the mapping of adress spaces to the logical names of primitives (for example https://github.com/bonfireprocessor/bonfire-soc/blob/master/soc.bmm). When passed to Xilinx synthesis it creates finally a „back annotated“ variant of it, which contains the actual location of the block RAMs, data2mem can only work with this.
Here the example: https://github.com/bonfireprocessor/bonfire-soc/blob/master/soc_bd.bmm
(Normally this version should not be part of a git repo, it is only there by accident...)

When creating a design manually with RTL you need to create the bmm manually, it is therefore input to FuseSOC. Some Xilinx tools like core generators may also create them.

With Vivado unfortunately things change completely, I have not get a similar process running.

Hello there,

I have come with some first attempt to be able to add bookmarks (and took the chance to add a helper method to add metadata that's currently possible already)

Since I don't know anything about the PDF spec all I did was basically to copy the format used by PyPDF2 to add the outlines objects and add this to pdfrw Writer class.

Added a addBookmark method similar to the one from PyPDF2, although it is lacking additional options like zoom or XYZ values that doesn't seem important neither.
Since I didn't want to modify the original Writer, I extended the class which means some additional code was needed but if this was to be added into the original class, some code can be removed.

Any improvement or anything that looks odd? It seems to work really well, indeed the small layer on top of the PDF spec is really useful!

[pdfrw_bookmarks.zip](https://github.com/pmaupin/pdfrw/files/692372/pdfrw_bookmarks.zip)


Here's the sample code, and attached the code and a test.

```
class NewPdfWriter(PdfWriter):

    def __init__(self, version='1.3', compress=False):
        self._bookmarks = []
        self._bookmarksDict = {}
        self._info = None

        super(NewPdfWriter, self).__init__(version, compress)
        
    def addBookmark(self, title, pageNum, parent = None):
        '''
        Adds a new bookmark entry.
        pageNum must be a valid page number in the writer
        and parent can be a bookmark object returned by a previous addBookmark call
        '''
                
        try:
            page = self.pagearray[pageNum]
        except IndexError:
            # TODO: Improve error handling ?
            PdfOutputError("Invalid page number: " % (pageNum))
            
        bookmark = {
            'title': title,
            'page': page,
            'childs': []
        }
        bid = id(bookmark)        
        
        if not parent:            
            self._bookmarks.append(bookmark)        
            
        else:
            parentObj = self._bookmarksDict.get(id(parent), None)
            if not parentObj:
                PdfOutputError("Bookmark parent object not found: " % parent)
                        
            parentObj['childs'].append(bookmark)
                        
        self._bookmarksDict[bid] = bookmark
        return bookmark
        
    def setInfo(self, info):
        '''
        Sets pdf metadata, info must be a dict where each key is the metadata key
        standard/known keys are:
            Title
            Author
            Creator
            Producer
        '''
        self._info = info
        
    def write(self, fname, trailer=None, user_fmt=None, disable_gc=True):        
            
        # Recursive function to build outlines tree
        def buildOutlines(parent, bookmarks):
            
            outline = None
            
            if bookmarks:
                outline = IndirectPdfDict()
                outline.Count = len(bookmarks)
                
                first = None
                next = None
                last = None
                                       
                for b in bookmarks:
                    
                    newb = IndirectPdfDict(
                        Parent = parent or outline,
                        Title = b['title'],
                        A = IndirectPdfDict(
                            D = PdfArray( (b['page'], PdfName('Fit')) ),
                            S = PdfName('GoTo')
                        )
                    ) 
                    
                    if not first:
                        first = newb
                        
                    else:
                        last.Next = newb
                        newb.Prev = last                                              
                        
                    last = newb
                    
                    # Add children, if any.
                    if b['childs']:
                        childOutline = buildOutlines(newb, b['childs'])
                        newb.First = childOutline.First
                        newb.Last = childOutline.Last
                        newb.Count = childOutline.Count
                        
                        
                outline.First = first
                outline.Last = last
                     
            return outline
            
        # Testing for now, only add root level bookmarks
        outlines = buildOutlines(None, self._bookmarks)
                       
        # If not custom trailer is given and we have info to set.
        # set info on self trailer
        if not trailer:
            if self._info:
                self.trailer.Info = IndirectPdfDict(**self._info)            
                
            if outlines:
                self.trailer.Root.Outlines = outlines
        
        
        # if user_fmt is given use it otherwise use default from pdfrw
        # this if is not necessary if this code is moved into the actual writer, did it this way
        # for now to avoid adding a reference to user_fmt
        if user_fmt:
            super(NewPdfWriter, self).write(fname, trailer, user_fmt, disable_gc)
        else:
            super(NewPdfWriter, self).write(fname, trailer, disable_gc=disable_gc)
```

Hi Sebastian,

since `mlxtend` is already deployed as a `pypi` package creating the conda recipe and binaries is (almost) trivial.

Make sure you have installed `conda-build` in your path, then you can run

```
conda skeleton pipy mlxtend
```

this will create a folder `mlxtend` with the file `meta.yaml` containing the conda recipe on it.

For some reason I didn't really debug, this seems to miss some of `mlxtend` requirements (namely `scikit-learn`, `pandas` and `matplotlib`), but you can just edit the `meta.yaml` file to include them:

```yaml
requirements:
  build:
    - python
    - setuptools
    - numpy >=1.10.4
    - scipy >=0.17
    - scikit-learn >=0.18
    - pandas >=0.17.1
    - matplotlib >=1.5.1
  run:
    - python
    - numpy >=1.10.4
    - scipy >=0.17
    - scikit-learn >=0.18
    - pandas >=0.17.1
    - matplotlib >=1.5.1
```

After this change, by running `conda build mlxtend` it will create the required binaries for your platform and give you its location (in my case, `/home/javier/anaconda3/conda-bld/linux-64/mlxtend-0.7.0-py36h6146f6b_0.tar.bz2`). Then create the binaries for all the other platforms with:

```
conda convert --platform all PATH/mlxtend-0.7.0-py36h6146f6b_0.tar.bz2 -o OUTPUT_PATH
```

which will create all the binaries and put them in `OUTPUT_PATH`.

Hi, 
When you define the Reader the Objectclass definition is needed to create the list of attributes available for that kind of entry. If you want to exclude a specific class when retrieving entries you can just use the query parameter of the Reader object:
```
from ldap3 import Reader, Connection
c = Connection('my_server', 'my_user', 'my_password)
c.bind()
r = Reader(c, 'person', 'o=my_base', '(&(objectClass=user)(!(objectClass=computer)))')
r.search()
```
So you will get back a cursor with the "person" objectClass definition (read from the server schema) and a list of entries that excludes entries with the "computer" objectClass.

Let me know if this solves your issue.

Bye,
Giovanni

Now, I might be missing something, but is there any way to extract "gallery order" for use in the filenames from Tumblr?  Right now, I can point gallery-dl at a tumblr post, and it gets all the pictures fine - but sometimes that means for short two-to-ten page comic posts the files are downloaded out of order.  After doing some testing and poking around it seems that the order that the files display in a photo post isn't necessarily the same as the order of their filenames (or the 'name' parameter).

You can test this yourself by creating a photo post, uploading say four photos one at a time, then saving the post.  Go back and edit the post, and drag photo 2 before photo 1, and save it again.  If you use CTRL+RIGHTCLICK+"View Image" on each in turn your tabs should go to filenames based off of _o2, _o1, _o3, _o4.  If you point gallery-dl at it it'll download it just fine, but the end result will sort as _o1, _o2, _o3, _o4. 
This will show the gallery in the wrong order, which is terrible for comic posts.
Unusually, gallery-dl seems to download them in _o2, _o1, _o3, _o4 order, (according to the on-screen status), but I can't see it exposing that order to the user in parameters anywhere.  


In addition to this, I don't see a way to extract the 'core name' of a file for use in the extractor.*.filename parameter.  Tumblr filenames when downloaded without a filename parameter come out something like
tumblr_fakeusername_12345678912o1.jpg
However, using the filename parameter to add extra stuff to the filename means you can't get that clean end anymore.  The closest parameter is 'name', which comes out something like

A problem I found with Jedi's ast (and I admit that I didn't play with it for very long, so there may already be a way around this) is that because it stores whitespace information (in order to be round-trippable), it's more challenging to do refactoring or anything that involves creating ast nodes and injecting them into the tree. I played around with this [once](https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/issues/103#issuecomment-99263760) and wasn't very satisfied with how things worked. For instance, it was hard to inject a node into a loop body because you'd have to figure out how to indent it. To me, for a nice roundtripping AST library this would "just work". 

Another nice thing to have would be for the library to be able to handle syntax for any (supported) version of Python, even ones other than the one that the code is running in. A common source of invalid bug reports to pyflakes comes from people complaining about syntax errors because they ran it in Python 2 and the code only works in Python 3 (or visa versa).

For webhook usage, please refer to the [webhook examples](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/tree/master/examples/webhook).

Simply put, instead of using `MessageLoop`, you use `OrderedWebhook`:
```python
from telepot.loop import OrderedWebhook

def handle(msg):
    # ......

bot = telepot.Bot(TOKEN)
webhook = OrderedWebhook(bot, handle)

webhook.run_as_thread()
```

And then, you have to make the webserver, on receiving some HTTP requests, feed data to the `OrderedWebhook` object. Using flask as an example, you may do something like this:
```python
from flask import Flask, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/webhook_path', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def pass_update():
    webhook.feed(request.data)
    return 'OK'
```

guessit (the library used to parse release names) can potentially return multiple values for several fields. Some fields are usually single value, but because of wrong parsing/guess or because of some uncommon scenario the result might be multiple values. E.g.: 
- you could get `BluRay` and `WEB-DL` in `format` field when parsing a release name (`abc.WEB.BD.1080p`) which refers to a pack of episodes. Guessit returns correctly multiple values. 
- you could wrongly get `BluRay` and `WEB-DL` for a strange release `abc s01e02 Strange Things on Web BLURAY 1080p`. Guessit wrongly returns multiple values. 

guessit parser is powerful and it is always improving, but it's not perfect. 

Since guessit could detect multiple values in some cases for certain properties, Subliminal needs to handle that. 

The other software you mentioned fixed the issue by adding an extra custom rule to guessit to discard and log multiple values on the `format` property. That way, guessit will never return more than one value for that property and this exception will never happen in subliminal. So, it's a workaround.

The emojione repository has a JSON file that contains shortcodes and other information on emoji they implement. Their shortcodes seem to mostly coincide with Discord's, so it may serve as a good resource:

It's possible to check for unread messages with PRAW, but I don't believe that Reddit's API supports sending the notification with each request. You have to check for notifications explicitly (see [get_unread()](https://praw.readthedocs.org/en/v2.1.21/pages/code_overview.html#praw.__init__.PrivateMessagesMixin.get_unread)). The two approaches that you could take would be:

1. Implement a timer as you suggested. Have it make a new request every 30 seconds or so.
2. Check for messages every time the user switches to a new page or refreshes the screen.
3. Some hybrid of 1 and 2, where every time the user opens a new page it checks how much time has elapsed.

I would advise against option 1 because it will involve spinning off threads that need to interact with the main program loop. This would be a huge pain to implement. Option 2 could work, the main issue is that each PRAW request takes 2 seconds, and you don't want to block the user from doing anything for an additional 2 seconds every time they load a page. You might have to get creative here.

This issue should now be solved...

---
**For future reference**: The problem turned out to be the code in the `parser-input-select.js` file which binds to *all* tables and listens for a "tablesorter-initialized" or "updateComplete" event. Once that happens, the code added more bindings for form element interactions (focus, blur, change, keyup, etc).

So, when the core plugin "triggered" a "tablesorter-initialized" on the table, it bubbled up through the table that was being used for layout. So now the layout table had event listeners and fired whenever the filter inputs changed... basically breaking a bunch of stuff since the filter inputs aren't contained in the same table's tbody.

The fix involved changing all `trigger` functions on the main table into `triggerHandler` which does not bubble up the DOM (https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/commit/87a10d69879abe9963d01c06c6c08627d34008a7).

Also, fixed was an issue in IE8 where the checkbox code needed to `return true` before IE8 would process the change (https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/commit/b84b38e6fab70a42c33f57dcabb96fba5361f91f).

So the big things to come to an agreement/decision on are…

1. Whether to move center/strip/just/etc. onto Sequence. At the moment, I'm leaning toward not doing it yet, releasing blessings 1.7 with your changes, and then doing the more difficult work of overloading all the `__add__` operators and such to return Sequences and calling that 2.0—essentially raising the level of abstraction of the whole library.
2. Spelling of the input methods. Here's a suggestion I'm 80% happy with. We rename cbreak() to single_key() (a name I like but don't love) and combine it with raw(), differentiating on an optional `raw=True` kwarg. We have it yield a callable that takes the place of inkey(). This keeps the user from trying to use inkey() outside of cbreak or raw mode, where it really makes no sense. It reduces the method count of Terminal by 2 and makes cbreak mode easier to stumble upon than the rarely-needed raw mode. Plus, as a bonus, it removes some code duplication.

    ```python
    with term.single_key() as keystroke:  # Other name ideas: keystroke_mode(), keys(), single_keypress(), keypresses(). Ideas welcome.
        for x in range(5):
            print keystroke()
    ```

Feel free to work on this if you wish, and I'm happy to take a look. This is off the top of my head, so this list may not be complete, but my requirements for merging to master include:

- graceful fallback if secp256k1 does not exist
- use the built-in ctypes (as opposed to cffi or something else not included in base python)
- allow users choice of which back-end to use
- not too horrible

To do this right, a lot of code in pycoin/ecdsa will have to be refactored. If you want to get something that works for you, I'd be grateful. There is a lot of ugly work with ctypes interface that will be easy to refactor once it's done.

It doesn't have a default value for the argument and it always sets both pointers to the new end of the file. And it returns an error for values greater than the current length, while Python allows that.

Or do you mean we can add some code around it to get Python's behavior?

We can certainly use `frames=None` as default argument and use `tell()` if `frames is None`.

But shall we *not* raise an error for values larger than the total length?
This would be a little strange (but possible), because we would have to check manually if we have to call `SFC_FILE_TRUNCATE` at all and if not, call `seek()` to move past the end of the file.

And do we really want to keep the read/write pointer unchanged?
Even if it ends up at a position beyond the end of the file?

For me its strange to write to a position far beyond the end of the file, but currently `seek()`ing works and "unwritten" frames seem to be filled with zeros, so probably that's OK?

> Are there any other pip packages out there that ship compiled code? This still seems troublesome to me.

Not that I am aware of. But simply shipping it as if it was some random binary file (which plenty of pip packages are doing) seems like a neatly simple solution to me: Other OSes just don't care and it potentially makes the installation on Windows less cumbersome. All that without requiring any special building and distributing on your side.

> If we do go down this route, we should ship OSX binaries as well. Linux users would probably prefer to use their package manager instead.

Thankfully there is Homebrew so there is no need for this. Both OSX and Linux are capable of installing the lib themselves (and it is second nature to devs on them to do so).

Sounds like a plan.

For eg
```python
def get_table_base_class(sort_url_name)
    class SortableTableBase(Table):
        def sort_url(self, col_key, reverse=False):
            if reverse:
                direction = 'desc'
            else:
                direction = 'asc'
            return url_for(sort_url_name, sort=col_key, direction=direction)
    return SortableTableBase

# then

SortableTable = (
    create_table(
        'SortableTable',  # you might want to make this value be different for different pages - might make debugging nicer
        base=get_table_base_class(sort_url_name='index'),  # or pass in some other url name here
        options=dict(
            allow_sort=True))
    .add_column('id', Col('ID'))
    .add_column('name', Col('Description'))
    .add_column('link', LinkCol('Link', 'flask_link', url_kwargs=dict(id='id'), allow_sort=False))
)
```

I guess it would suffice, though the way you describe it (as a field in the format object), it sounds like you could create multiple formats with the same name. I would rather require that format names be unique.

Yes, you could prevent duplicate names by using a custom setter, but to me that seems more convoluted than simply having a dictionary as another workbook attribute, to complement `workbook.formats`. It would have format names as the keys and the corresponding format objects are the values. Not every format would be required to have a dictionary entry (you could have "anonymous" formats).

The typical way to create a named format would thus be

    wb.add_format({'bold': True, 'font_color': 'red}, name='error')

(It would still return a reference to the created format, but I've thrown it away in the example.)

Of course, having a "named format" dictionary is not incompatible with having a name attribute within the format object, but I'm thinking it might be annoying to enforce synchronization between the two.

If there's going to be a getter method, I agree that `get_format()` is a better spelling. It could even work for both integers (via `workbook.formats[i]`) and strings.

Personally, I don't think it's too late to replace `get_worksheet_by_name()` with `get_worksheet()`. But I would also not be opposed to having both spellings.

To store the new values, the authorized file needs a new syntax while staying compatible with the previous syntax.

## Suggested syntax:
```
[allowed]:[hash]:[path]
```
Where `[allowed]` can either be `y` or `n`.
`[hash]` may be `SKIP` which allows/denies the file even when it was changed.

The filename is moved to the end so it can contain colons.

So a file might look like this:
```
y:074a866c606abbd2051c8ffad840e8d407c49823:/i/am/allowed/if/i/don't/change
n:3cb83fa2e43208ad89acabfa4e6678f8c09ff7a4:/i/am/denied/if/i/don't/change
y:SKIP:/i/am/always/allowed
n:SKIP:/i/am/always/denied
/we/still/support/the/old/syntax:334fc4857b38918a32cbdef9717ec807b7366811
```

I asked on the off chance that you might've remembered the details. There's no need to get back to a less stable implementation just to satisfy my curiosity. :)

I looked at Click's `click.Path`, and it defaults to using `resolve_path=False`, which means we need to add CWD ourselves (which is also used by the current tests, to exercise resolution from various directories). We could switch that to `True`, but that would also resolve symlinks ahead of time, which means the "basename" we send to the far side would be that of the target of the local link, not the source. I *think* users would prefer to use the source name, so that e.g. if I symlink "today.csv -> all_reports/2017-06-20.csv", and then do `wormhole send today.csv`, the recipient will store a file named `today.csv` and not `2017-06-20.csv`.

@contextlib.contextmanager
def fiscal_calendar(start_year=None, start_month=None, start_day=None):
    global START_YEAR, START_MONTH, START_DAY

    # use default values if arguments are omitted.
    start_year = START_YEAR if start_year is None else start_year
    start_month = START_MONTH if start_month is None else start_month
    start_day = START_DAY if start_day is None else start_day

    # Temporarily change global variables and then restore previous values
    old_start_year, old_start_month, old_start_day = START_YEAR, START_MONTH, START_DAY
    setup_fiscal_year(start_year, start_month, start_day)
    yield
    setup_fiscal_year(old_start_year, old_start_month, old_start_day)

1st to train dataset once:
`from math import sqrt
from sklearn import neighbors
from os import listdir
from os.path import isdir, join, isfile, splitext
import pickle
from PIL import Image, ImageFont, ImageDraw, ImageEnhance
import face_recognition
from face_recognition import face_locations
from face_recognition.cli import image_files_in_folder

ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS = {'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg'}

def train(train_dir, model_save_path = "", n_neighbors = None, knn_algo = 'ball_tree', verbose=False):
    """
    Trains a k-nearest neighbors classifier for face recognition.

    :param train_dir: directory that contains a sub-directory for each known person, with its name.

     (View in source code to see train_dir example tree structure)

     Structure:
        <train_dir>/
        ├── <person1>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   ├── <somename2>.jpeg
        │   ├── ...
        ├── <person2>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   └── <somename2>.jpeg
        └── ...
    :param model_save_path: (optional) path to save model of disk
    :param n_neighbors: (optional) number of neighbors to weigh in classification. Chosen automatically if not specified.
    :param knn_algo: (optional) underlying data structure to support knn.default is ball_tree
    :param verbose: verbosity of training
    :return: returns knn classifier that was trained on the given data.
    """
    X = []
    y = []
    for class_dir in listdir(train_dir):
        if not isdir(join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            continue
        for img_path in image_files_in_folder(join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            image = face_recognition.load_image_file(img_path)
            faces_bboxes = face_locations(image)
            if len(faces_bboxes) != 1:
                if verbose:
                    print("image {} not fit for training: {}".format(img_path, "didn't find a face" if len(faces_bboxes) < 1 else "found more than one face"))
                continue
            X.append(face_recognition.face_encodings(image, known_face_locations=faces_bboxes)[0])
            y.append(class_dir)


    if n_neighbors is None:
        n_neighbors = int(round(sqrt(len(X))))
        if verbose:
            print("Chose n_neighbors automatically as:", n_neighbors)

    knn_clf = neighbors.KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=n_neighbors, algorithm=knn_algo, weights='distance')
    knn_clf.fit(X, y)

    if model_save_path != "":
        with open(model_save_path, 'wb') as f:
            pickle.dump(knn_clf, f)
    return knn_clf

if __name__ == "__main__":
    knn_clf = train("knn_examples/train", "knn_examples/train/model/model.txt")`

And 2nd to get result:
`from math import sqrt
from sklearn import neighbors
from os import listdir
from os.path import isdir, join, isfile, splitext
import pickle
from PIL import Image, ImageFont, ImageDraw, ImageEnhance
import face_recognition
from face_recognition import face_locations
from face_recognition.cli import image_files_in_folder

ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS = {'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg'}

def predict(X_img_path, knn_clf = None, model_save_path ="knn_examples/train/model/model.txt", DIST_THRESH = .45):
    """
    recognizes faces in given image, based on a trained knn classifier

    :param X_img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param knn_clf: (optional) a knn classifier object. if not specified, model_save_path must be specified.
    :param model_save_path: (optional) path to a pickled knn classifier. if not specified, model_save_path must be knn_clf.
    :param DIST_THRESH: (optional) distance threshold in knn classification. the larger it is, the more chance of misclassifying an unknown person to a known one.
    :return: a list of names and face locations for the recognized faces in the image: [(name, bounding box), ...].
        For faces of unrecognized persons, the name 'N/A' will be passed.
    """

    if not isfile(X_img_path) or splitext(X_img_path)[1][1:] not in ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS:
        raise Exception("invalid image path: {}".format(X_img_path))

    if knn_clf is None and model_save_path == "":
        raise Exception("must supply knn classifier either thourgh knn_clf or model_save_path")

    if knn_clf is None:
        with open(model_save_path, 'rb') as f:
            knn_clf = pickle.load(f)

    X_img = face_recognition.load_image_file(X_img_path)
    X_faces_loc = face_locations(X_img)
    if len(X_faces_loc) == 0:
        return []

    faces_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(X_img, known_face_locations=X_faces_loc)


    closest_distances = knn_clf.kneighbors(faces_encodings, n_neighbors=1)

    is_recognized = [closest_distances[0][i][0] <= DIST_THRESH for i in range(len(X_faces_loc))]

    # predict classes and cull classifications that are not with high confidence
    return [(pred, loc) if rec else ("N/A", loc) for pred, loc, rec in zip(knn_clf.predict(faces_encodings), X_faces_loc, is_recognized)]

def draw_preds(img_path, preds):
    """
    shows the face recognition results visually.

    :param img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param preds: results of the predict function
    :return:
    """
    source_img = Image.open(img_path).convert("RGBA")
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(source_img)
    for pred in preds:
        loc = pred[1]
        name = pred[0]
        # (top, right, bottom, left) => (left,top,right,bottom)
        draw.rectangle(((loc[3], loc[0]), (loc[1],loc[2])), outline="red")
        draw.text((loc[3], loc[0] - 30), name, font=ImageFont.truetype('Pillow/Tests/fonts/FreeMono.ttf', 30))
    source_img.show()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    
    for img_path in listdir("knn_examples/test"):
        preds = predict(join("knn_examples/test", img_path), model_save_path="knn_examples/train/model/model.txt")
        print(preds)
        draw_preds(join("knn_examples/test", img_path), preds)`

The handler tries to store a log file in a blob container when the log file is outdated, and the handler creates the container at the same time if it doesn't exist in your storage.

The handler also evaluates if the log file should be rolled over or not when logging is performed, so add some logging to the file at the last of your example like the following and run it once.

    logging.getLogger('root').info('test message')

And then run the example again after 24 hours or more (because the interval is set to 1 day in the example), and the container will be created in your storage and the outdated log file will be stored in the container.

Sure @Paul424, mostly I ran into the issue when using `modeltranslation` with `django-filter`. `django-filter` operates by taking a list of fields and generating a list of possible values to filter the field by, for example if you specify `relatedobj__name` as a filter field it will get a list of values to generate the filter.

The work around was to subclass the class from `django_filter` and add a hook to look for specific field names that have translations and change the field name based on `get_language()`. Took a while to work out the issue and then how to change the third party package to add in the override.

Are you an admin, or a co-admin? I noticed this in the API documentation for `As-User`:

> Note: Admins can use As-User to impersonate any managed users including co-admins. Co-admins can use As-User to impersonate managed users, but cannot impersonate any admin or co-admin. A 403 Forbidden error will be returned.

Are you a co-admin, and is the "Ned Stark" user another admin or co-admin?

If you're not sure, you can do ``print client.user(user_id='me').get(fields=['role']).role`` and ``print user.get(fields=['role']).role``.

You may need to do something like this:

```python
ADMIN_ROLE = 'admin'
COADMIN_ROLE = 'coadmin'

...

my_role = me.get(fields=['role']).role
am_coadmin = my_role == COADMIN_ROLE

...

for user in users:
    try:
        main_folder = client.as_user(user).folder(folder_id='0').get()
    except BoxAPIException as box_api_exception:
        if (box_api_exception.status == 403) and am_coadmin and (user.get(fields=['role']).role in (ADMIN_ROLE, COADMIN_ROLE)):
            continue
        raise
    ...
```

One of the reasons the input was being processed was because the default processor was causing so many issues but it seemed (from a previous comment) that they did not want to change it. I moved the defaults into variables at the top to make them more visible and easier to replace because I thought that it might be necessary. I don't really like the WRatio scorer as it gives some inconsistent results depending on string lengths but I didn't feel that I could go and change it or the default processor at the time.

It does make sense not to process the query for the use case you've given. For the use case I had clearing up both query AND choices was more useful (my use was changing characters with diacritical marks to those without). It also followed the existing design somewhat - before my changes if you didn't specify a scorer but did specify a processor your processor would be ignored (WRatio runs full process on query and choice).

Whatever the outcome I would suggest there should be an example added to the readme to make the behaviour clear.

FWIW, here's a modified excerpt from our repo's README about IE that I wrote:

## Configure Environment

1. Download the appropriate browser drivers:
  * For **Google Chrome** - the latest Chromedriver executable [can be found here](https://sites.google.com/a/chromium.org/chromedriver/downloads). As of this writing, it was [version 2.21](http://chromedriver.storage.googleapis.com/index.html?path=2.21/). The file will always be named `chromedriver_win32.zip`.
  * For **Microsoft Internet Explorer**, you _may_ select the appropriate bit version for your workstation (almost certainly the 64-bit version) from [the Selenium HQ site](http://www.seleniumhq.org/download/). As of this writing, [the latest 64-bit version was 2.53.1](http://selenium-release.storage.googleapis.com/2.48/IEDriverServer_x64_2.53.1.zip). _However_, there is [a known bug](https://code.google.com/p/selenium/issues/detail?id=5116) that causes sending keys (to emulate a user typing) to be extremely slow.  Therefore, as of this writing, <u>it is recommended you use the 32-bit version of the driver</u> (version [2.53.1 here](http://selenium-release.storage.googleapis.com/2.48/IEDriverServer_Win32_2.53.1.zip))
  * **Mozilla Firefox** does not require an external driver - it comes with Selenium "hooks" built in.
2. Unzip the file(s) and place the executable(s) (`chromedriver.exe` and/or `IEDriverServer.exe`) somewhere that it makes sense to put in your Path system variable. [Here are instructions on how to add a path](http://www.computerhope.com/issues/ch000549.htm) so that is accessible system-wide. The author's recommendation is to create a directory named `bin` at the root of the **C** drive. This allows you to add other simple executables in the same directory, which can be invoked from then on (once you add this directory to the Path).

### Special Instructions for Internet Explorer

Assuming you have already set up the driver for Internet Explorer (see above), there are two additional steps you need to take in order to have IE function as a web client for testing:

1. You must launch Internet Explorer and verify that the zoom level is set to exactly 100%. For instructions on how to verify that, [see this page](http://www.thewindowsclub.com/change-zoom-level-in-internet-explorer).
2. You must have **Protected Settings** <u>enabled</u> for *all* security zones. [An article here will explain in more detail](http://jimevansmusic.blogspot.com/2012/08/youre-doing-it-wrong-protected-mode-and.html), but the salient setting is depicted in this picture:

![Internet Options Security Settings](http://4.bp.blogspot.com/-ZQ0fWXNfyv8/UDKB_lZ4RCI/AAAAAAAAACM/TAiNFoZRbUc/s1600/OptionsDlgEdit.png)

How you do it?

This is a support question
and it almost falls out of the scope of scrapyd.
But it's the kind of question that should be answered by FAQ
though rather in [scrapyd-client](https://github.com/scrapy/scrapyd-client)
which recently detached from scrapy.

The answer is in setuptools.
The scrapy deploy command creates a setup.py from a template
and this template includes only the modules it can find in the scrapy project
(scrapy project as defined in `scrapy.cfg`)

One way is to put settings on the top level, next to `scrapy.cfg`
where it will ignored by the default (generated) `setup.py`

You could put then such a block in you original settings
```python
try:
    import dev_settings
    from dev_settings import *
except ImportError:
    pass
```
and a file `dev_settings.py` on the top level.

dev_settings will be available to your modules
because scrapy adds the top level directory to the import path.
However when packaging the egg for scrapyd, they will be ignored.
Only modules inside your project are packaged by the default `setup.py`.

You can also write/modify `setup.py` yourself to exclude dev_settings.
This has the advantage of more control over your excluded files,
eg, you can place `dev_settings.py` next to `settings.py`, inside the project dir
or to answer your original question,
one would probably put settings.py on the top level
and update `scrapy.cfg` (and `setup.py` if generated) to know where to find the settings.

if you succeed on the second, untried, scenario and mind sharing how you do it,
let us close this issue and come up with a doc PR to scrapyd-client.

Have fun experimenting.

The memory problem is caused by the size of `descs` that becomes >12 GB for an input image of 9 Mpix with 18 gradient orientations. This cannot be fixed with more aggressive garbage collection.

Some considerations:
- I think an image of size 9 Mpix is _huge_ for computer vision. Maybe you can scale down your image size?
- 18 gradient orientations are quite a lot compared to most other descriptors. You should be able to get decent performance with fewer orientations.
- A memory footprint of 13 GB doesn't really seem that big a deal to me for scientific computing applications.

Therefore, I don't think the implementation of `daisy()` should be made more memory efficient by processing the input image in chunks as this would increase code complexity. Rather, I think you should subdivide your input image and call `daisy()` for each image chunk.

Best,
Anders

Sorry to fiddle with the weights again, but the changes in #dd5a708 are necessary to give a consistent level of denoising regardless of the number of image dimensions.

To demonstrate that the present implementation is correct, see the following example, which I repeated for the `_denoise_tv_chambolle_nd` code prior to and after #dd5a708
```python
import numpy as np
from skimage import restoration, data, color, img_as_float
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(1234)
astro = img_as_float(data.astronaut())  # [:128, :128])
astro_gray = color.rgb2gray(astro)
img = astro_gray.copy()
# add noise to astronaut
sigma = 0.15
img += sigma * np.random.standard_normal(img.shape)
# clip noise so that it does not exceed allowed range for float images.
img = np.clip(img, 0, 1)


# generate 3D, 4D and 8D images by tiling
img3 = np.tile(img[..., None], (1, 1, 16));
img4 = np.tile(img[..., None, None], (1, 1, 4, 4));
img8 = np.tile(img[..., None, None, None, None, None, None], (1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2));

# denoise all nD images and view a single 2D slice
w = 0.2
denoised_astro2 = _denoise_tv_chambolle_nd(img, weight=w, multichannel=False)
denoised_astro3 = _denoise_tv_chambolle_nd(img3, weight=w, multichannel=False)
denoised_astro4 = _denoise_tv_chambolle_nd(img4, weight=w, multichannel=False)
denoised_astro8 = _denoise_tv_chambolle_nd(img8, weight=w, multichannel=False)


fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2)
axes[0, 0].imshow(denoised_astro2, cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
axes[0, 0].set_axis_off()
axes[0, 0].set_title('2D')
axes[0, 1].imshow(denoised_astro3[..., 0], cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
axes[0, 1].set_axis_off()
axes[0, 1].set_title('3D')
axes[1, 0].imshow(denoised_astro4[..., 0, 0], cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
axes[1, 0].set_axis_off()
axes[1, 0].set_title('4D')
axes[1, 1].imshow(denoised_astro8[..., 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0], cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
axes[1, 1].set_axis_off()
axes[1, 1].set_title('8D')
volshow([denoised_astro2, denoised_astro3[..., 0],  denoised_astro4[..., 0, 0],
         denoised_astro8[..., 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]])

```


With the old code, the level of denoising decreases as the number of images increases:
![tv_old_nd_weightings](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/12531186/3a50b7a2-c1c2-11e5-8a44-7f1cb521b34b.png)


The new code, gives much more consistent results:
![tv_new_nd_weightings](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/12531187/3ebc3802-c1c2-11e5-802a-58645859134a.png)

@odebeir thank you for spotting this bug. There is a comment saying in the source code of the cython function
```
   # if image.max() is 0, then dist_scale can have an unverified value
   # and color_lut[<int>(dist * dist_scale)] may cause a segmentation fault
   # so we verify we have a positive image and that the max is not 0.0.
```
but in fact we only check for strictly negative values
```
if image.min() < 0.0:
        raise ValueError("Image must contain only positive values")
```
So this should we replaced by 
```
if image.min() <= 0.0:
        raise ValueError("Image must contain only positive values")
```

@subutai the KNN can definitely use some cleaning up. Started to collect some of the issues last summer (#2271, #2270, #2172), which can use some reorganization when we decide to actually clean it up.

> How about combining the classes TemporalMemory (or TP?) and SpatialPooler in a way that realizes @fergalbyrne 's idea? 

I was quite favor of combining spatial and temporal classes a time ago but I changed my mind. As a class evoluate or even is replaced then all combined super classes also need be updated (look the old spatial pooler [AKA `FDRCSpatial2`] case, for example). Furthermore, we would need have several combined classes to attend each implementation (for example, Comb1=SP+TM_v1, Comb2=SP+TM_v2, etc). Because this, Network class is essential. I'm favor of keep implementations in a independent way in order to avoid such additional maintenance work BUT make such implementations use the same shared abstract model of objects to store states of Columns, Cells, Segments, etc. This way we could change the spatial and temporal implementations but without change the structure of such neuronal model of objects. In my opinion, Connections class is a first step in this path.

It seems that for some reason old android versions (4.1.2 for me) can't dynamically load .so libraries. A bare python is launching for the reason that it is forced to load in  [PythonUtil.java](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/master/pythonforandroid/bootstraps/sdl2/build/src/org/kivy/android/PythonUtil.java).

As for 05.02.2017 it is written so that when python2.7 fails to load it tries to load python3 module which doesn't exist for python2 app and throw exception for python3 module. With this behaviour it is hard to debug the problem when python2.7 module is not loading.

I suggest [pull request ](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/pull/1007) which can help people with old android devices to realise the problem.

P.S. at the moment I don't know how to make old android to load libraries other way than force them to load in PythonUtil.java

> I'd say that the on-screen-overwritten text has to be logged as well.

To recap, this is exactly what `script` does. Maybe the best value/investment ratio is just to configure your Guake to launch `script` instead of the default shell, or rather, a tiny wrapper in front of `script` that logs into a file with a unique name. This wrapper functionality could perhaps be integrated into Guake.

> writing whatever non-control characters are flowing [...] maybe an option to disable control characters filtering.

Control character filtering is not available in `script`, and would require support from VTE. I find this of limited usefulness, you'd lose the information how the cursor is moved in order to overwrite, you'd only see the letters themselves leaving absolutely no clue what they overwrite. The result, in case of e.g. an interactive command line being edited, might be an absolutely meaningless mess. It might be useful for the regular output/error of most commands, though.

If you begin to filter out control characters (that is, begin to _interpret_ the data stream to some extent), it's already a tough question to what extent to interpret it. E.g. there's a REP escape sequence to repeat the previous character a certain number of times, would this be just silently swallowed or would that letter be repeated that many times in the logs? Sometimes a mapping is used, e.g. lowercase letters are converted into box drawing characters, if enabling/disabling this mode is filtered out then should the conversion be done for the log files or should the original letters appear there? These occurred to me right now, but there might be more.

> An advanced option could be to split STDOUT and STDERR to different files.

This probably cannot be implemented technically. In order to separate these two file descriptors, they must be separate file descriptors for the app that prints to them. (If they point to the same file or same terminal device, there's no way to tell which one the data was printed to.)

However, if they are distinguished from each other at some point, there's no way to guarantee the ordering of data. E.g. if an app writes something to stdout and then immediately afterwards something to stderr, whoever reads them cannot tell which one is the newer, potentially resulting in the order of them being swapped, and as a final result, something incorrect appearing in the terminal screen.

Even if it was implementable, its usefulness would also be questionable for me. If you run a single utility, it's okay to redirect stdout here and stderr there for whatever purposes, and then that goes for the execution of that single command only. If done for the entire terminal session, where let's say you run `gcc` twice, you wouldn't be able to pair up the two log files and figure out which lines of stdout come from the same run as which lines of stderr.

I agree that the user should be able to specify an altitude above sea level for the starting level of the grid and that the algorithm should correct for the difference between this altitude and the altitudes of the Radar objects which are being gridded.  I still think that the default value should be the altitude of the first radar.

A bit more explanation of what the code current does.  In the Grid classes axes attribute there are two dictioaries, **alt** and **z_disp** which record the altitude of the origin of the grid and the displacement from this origin.  To get the altitude above mean sea level for each point in the grid one must add the value found in **alt** dictionary to the values found in **z_disp** dictionary.  This is why line 136 in the `grid_from_radars` function sets **alt** to the altitude of the first radar, this is considered the origin of the grid.

In the gridding algorithm the altitudes of all the radars are assumed to be the same, this assumption is not correct and any displacements in altitude between the radars should be taken into account which is why there is a XXX comment on line 445.  If/when the option to specify the altitude of the radar is added then offsets for all radars altitudes to this value should be calculated and the z gate locations should be corrected in line 455 in the same manner as is done for the y and x gate locations.

Off-topic, but my issue is less about time and more about code ownership. Best to keep things separated to avoid potential problems down the line.

On-topic: I've upgraded to the latest and the same issue exists, with an RDATE being serialized with time information using the user's local machine settings, converted to UTC, and all the rest.

I propose, and aim to work on:

* Change the frontend widget/s so that it/they serialize dates as bare dates, without adding time data of any kind. Unless someone else wants to add a widget that collects time data, which I don't think is necessary, then any JS code calculating time can be stripped down.
* Allow backend to store `datetime.date` objects in the form `'RDATE:YYYYMMDD'`, and de-serializing this format back to `datetime.date` objects.
* If a naive datetime is serialized, it should be localized on server local timezone settings if present (UTC if not). If it is aware already, pass.
* Serialize datetime using the second format described in [RFC 2445](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2445.txt) (Section "4.8.5.3"), which uses the `TZID` property to identify the timezone by name.
  * Continue to support reading the first format, which is already in use, for backwards compatibility.
* De-serialize datetimes as timezone-aware by attaching the same timezone originally serialized (perform no implicit conversion to server local timezone)
  * Optionally add a property on the recurrence field for "utc_rdates" that presents the list of rdates converted to UTC (as a read-only property).

All that and hopefully nothing blows up. :)

@falseen 如果用的是AEAD模式，会尝试加载libsodium，用sodium_increment 来做nonce的递增，如果没有就用aead.py 里python实现的nonce_increment，这是参考了libev版的做法，不过libev版必须依赖sodium_increment。

aes-256-gcm 是使用openssl的加密库做加解密，但nonce递增会尝试用libsodium的sodium_increment。
sodium:aes-256-gcm 是使用libsodium的加密库做加解密。

我之前把mbedtls加密库也集成进来了，不过没有发pull request。

各个库的对应加密算法是可以混用的，也就是说服务器端可以配置成sodium:aes-256-gcm，客户端用aes-256-gcm或者mbedtls:aes-256-gcm。因为不同加密库在不同cpu下性能不一样，在支持sodium:aes-256-gcm的CPU下，sodium:aes-256-gcm是最快的，openssl的慢30%左右，mbedtls的gcm最慢，可能只有sodium的一半速度。

在arm设备下可能mbedtls快点，服务器端intel cpu不建议用mbedtls

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L313 is some Rust code which returns an `ExecuteProcessResult` from an `ExecuteProcessRequest`. It invokes a Python constructor here https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L321

We should add a new Python type, `FallibleExecuteProcessResponse`, pass its constructor along the same paths as we currently pass `ExecuteProcessResponse`.

Then we should add a new else-if clause which checks that `self.product()` is the new `FallibleExecuteProcessResponse` type. If so, it should do exactly what the current code does. Then we should modify the existing code to return a `future::err(Failure::Throw)`.

Unfortunately, the paths required to create a new type which is returned by the Rust code are a bit complex and verbose. We'll probably need to:
1. Add a new `FallibleExecuteProcessResult` datatype to https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/python/pants/engine/isolated_process.py
2. Modify `scheduler_create` in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/rust/engine/src/lib.rs to take a new `TypeConstraint` and a new constructor, so that the Rust code knows how to construct the Python type.
3. Add a new `constraint_fallible_execute_process_result` arg to `new_scheduler` (the python code which calls that Rust code from step 2) in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/python/pants/engine/native.py and pass it around everywhere that we pass around `constraint_execute_process_result`.
4. Pass `FallibleExecuteProcessResult` to populate the value `fallible_execute_process_result` in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/src/python/pants/engine/scheduler.py everywhere that we do the same for `ExecuteProcessResult`
5. For each place that we manually `raise ProcessExecutionFailure` https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/tests/python/pants_test/engine/test_isolated_process.py remove it because the underlying Rust should now be doing that.
6. Add a new test to https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdb81f1891493761d0f1e6ccedc4537b54af6f6b/tests/python/pants_test/engine/test_isolated_process.py which tests that we can request a `FallibleExecuteProcessResult` which works for both 0 and non-0 exit codes.

@za-arthur Any progress?  I think the only thing to resolve is how to name the stringdefs for the letters.

Also, a couple of recent changes that affect this patch:

I've made some changes to make it easier to add a new stemming algorithm - now you only need to add the new stemmer data to snowball-data (already done), add an entry to `libstemmer/modules.txt`, and add the new stemmer as `algorithms/nepali.sbl`.

There's also now more explicit support for Unicode - instead of:

```
stringdef DEVANAGARI_SIGN_CANDRABINDU hex '0901'
```

you should now use:

```
stringdef DEVANAGARI_SIGN_CANDRABINDU '{U+0901}'
```

A secondary note on why positional choices are odd: they can be expressed as a level in the command tree.
That is, there's not much difference between ` create gcp|gcs` and `gcp|gcs create`.

Phrased the other way around, every command in a structured CLI like this is a positional choice, so treating them as parameters is a little confusing.

In some cases it does make sense to auto-provision the identity, which is why we've added the option to turn on that behavior.

In some situations, since the CLI is mainly intended to be an interactive tool for use by people, it can lead to a bad experience if we always auto-provision identities. Specifically the case where you inadvertently type an incorrect identity username into a command--with the auto-provisioning behavior turned on you wouldn't get any feedback that you didn't end up doing what you intended.

For users that are going to automate or script with the CLI they may decide to always use the `--provision-identity` option.

@mingwandroid has it right that `CONDA_FORCE_32BIT` will be going away.  Instead, the override behavior will be explicitly setting desired subdirs.  Right now in `4.3.latest` I think it's `CONDA_SUBDIR=win-32` would usually work on windows.  In the future, the more general case will be something like `CONDA_SUBDIRS=win-32,noarch`, and we'll also be working on reducing package cache collisions between both channels and subdirs.  Right now, to avoid package cache churn, you can also set `CONDA_PKGS_DIRS`, which is now independent and decoupled from `CONDA_ENVS_DIRS`.

Hi. Here are my thoughts on your comments:

> Do you think it reasonable to add some VUnit specific stuff to an UVM test bench or do you think VUnit needs to run unmodified UVM test benches for it to be useful? Since it seems UVM does not standardize test pass/fail and leaves it up to the user it seems we should add a standardized mechanism for it.

In general, its to be noted that the HDL part (tb_top.sv) of SystemVerilog (which also contains the instance of the design) is not OOP-based. This mean that every user will have to write additional Vunit-specific code within the top module for every testbench used. I would prefer writing a single adapter/layer which can be re-used for all class-based OOP testbenches like UVM, and of course Python is the ideal candidate for that code. I am also a fan of having testbench-code independent of the flow/process in case the project decides to move to another tool. However, maybe its possible to put the Vunit-specific UVM-code into a seperate SystemVerilog module/class/scope keeping the rest of the TB code intact (for example using a second tb_top module). Let me try out some options

> By the way does UVM want test cases to run in individual simulations or in the same simulation?

Each testcase runs in an individual simulation

> Regarding test case discovery it would be convenient if test case names can be extracted robustly via some simple parsing of the verilog files such that the test case names are known without running any simulation. This is to make for example the --list command work or the per-test-case configuration of generics. 

I would like the --list to work too. Functionally, I would define a testcase as a combination of the test-class (the argument specified with +UVM_TESTNAME) and seed. Remember that the test-class is usually run over several seeds, sometimes thousands, and these seed values may not be known prior to launching the regression run. I would like Vunit to recognize a test-class+seed combination as a single testcase, so that I can get dedicated simulation results, debug waveforms, etc. There's no way I can pre-calculate all possible seed values for each testcase and specify them with compile-time HDL code. Is there someway I can declare only the test-classes in the SystemVerilog code (so the --list works for example) and then specify seed values dynamically during simulation time through the python code ?


> Regarding pass fail mechanism it is not good practise to just rely on the absence of errors to say a test passed. There must also be a completion message for a test to be considered a pass. In VUnit this means that any test that does not finish and call the cleanup code will fail. Is there something similar in the UVM world where the test must reach the final statement for a test to be deemed sucessful?

There is no completion message explicitly needed in UVM. Its all handled implicitly deep within the base-classes. The testcase is only responsible for creating and sending the stimuli and does not usually check anything. The checking mechanism is done passively by a dedicated component(s) which monitor transactions and issue error messages. 
UVM fundamental (you can read about UVM phasing and Objections on the UVM reference)
Once the test-case simulation is launched and the run_test() task is called, the testbench is brought through several so-called 'UVM phases'. In the UVM methodology, each component in the testbench is allowed to 'raise an objection' which needs to be eventually 'lowered' in-order for the entire testbench simulation to move from one-phase to the next. All the UVM components run in parallel with the test-case code, and after the test-case run_phase() task (where all the sequences/stimuli are executed) is complete (or if a timeout is triggered), the clean-up phases are automatically executed where the number of errors (messages) can also be fetched, and a summary of different message types is displayed. The final phase can be reached only after all the previous phases are successfully completed (either with or without error messages). Only after that is the $finish system task called. In case of a timeout trigger, the clean-up phase will register and display a UVM_ERROR message which mentions the timeout information. 
SUMMARY: Regardless of any error type or timeout, the clean-up phases will always be executed. After that and before the final phase, the error count can be obtained and an absense of errors implies a PASS.

Echoing @kgudel's point on behalf of [Pelias](https://github.com/pelias). It's great (and critical) that OA be a place to get "Authoritative Data".

But it's also then a burden that we pass onto any other consumers of the data, particularly those who don't have the knowledge or resources to do their own analysis or processing to make more sense of it. Plus the OpenAddresses project, by being a collective effort already, is in a great place to pull together knowledge of how to interpret different address formats from the downstream users, and it would be a shame for that to go to waste.

I'm a fan of @NelsonMinar's proposal (#283) for there to be the "Authoritative" files (`out.csv`) and then for there to be an additional _enhance_ step, with `enhanced` versions that pull together the OA interpretation from its downstream consumers (where it makes sense to do so). That way we can all use the authoritative data, but pool what we know about making sense of it.

Imagine you want to process the complete body posted to a server [1].
You can of course build the handler using `readany`, and buffering the content by hand until you've built the complete bytestring – probably using BytesIO or some kind of buffer – but that's overly complicated and completely unneeded: I want the complete body sent to the handler, i expect i can get it with `content.read()`. Handling buffering and reconstructing the body from its chunks is the job of the http server and that's exactly what `content.read()` is supposed to do.

Except calling `content.read()` need to duplicate the complete body three time in memory, which is completely inefficient and definitely not needed.

[1] In case you don't believe such use case exist: imagine a server receiving a zipfile as the body of the request, and then doing something with it.

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

Yeah, I'm not sure what ipython (or python) could reasonably do differently here.

As long as you have that `tasks` variable sitting around, you *might* go and do `tasks[0].result()` or `await tasks[0]` or something, which is how you propagate the exception further. So python has to hold on to the exception quietly and wait for you to do that.

When you shut down the interpreter, then that implicitly does `del tasks`, the reference count on the future objects drops to 0, they get garbage collected, and at that point their `__del__` method prints that "uh-oh there was an error that you never propagated, and now you never will because I'm being garbage collected!" message. But you can't really raise the error from inside `__del__`.

If you weren't capturing the `tasks` in a global variable and thus pinning them in memory, then the results here would be a little less confusing :-).

On Wednesday, 18 July 2018, Nick Harding <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> OK. It's actually reasonably simple- nothing to do with dtypes- false
> alarm!
> It's caused by having 2 identical cells in a row of the mapping array. In
> this case -1, -1.
> But can be recreated with 0, 0 etc.
>
Actually I think the fundamental problem is that the
AlleleCountsArray.map_alleles() method does not handle missing alleles (-1)
at all. I.e., the current implementation assumes no missing alleles. The
current implementation uses numpy fancy indexing, but I think we'd need to
code something up manually to handle the missing allele case, probably in
the model.pyx cython extension. Not quite the same, but for reference
here's the map_alleles implementation for a haplotype array:
https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/opt/model.pyx#L307

If there are repeated values in the mapping array that will also cause
strange behaviour, again because numpy fancy indexing is being used.
Repeated values shouldn't ever happen except for missing alleles (-1), it
would mean two alleles in the source are being mapped to the same allele in
the destination. However it might be worth handling this better if it does
occur.

Yeah, ideally tests should run in total isolation. The reason why your client fixture must be function scoped is that the event loop fixture is function scoped. You can override the event loop fixture to be session scoped though. I'm not sure this is tested but it should be possible :)

Make a conftest.py file in your tests directory, and put this in it:

```
@pytest.yield_fixture(scope='session')
def event_loop(request):
    """Create an instance of the default event loop for each test case."""
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy().new_event_loop()
    yield loop
    loop.close()
```

I hacked up a script plug-in (simpleHttp only) in a separate package, available at [git://git.silbe.org/letsencrypt-script-plugin.git](git://git.silbe.org/letsencrypt-script-plugin.git). It can be used like this:
`letsencrypt --authenticator letsencrypt-script-plugin:script --letsencrypt-script-plugin:script-auth-script custom-provision-script <other letsencrypt options>`

Apparently at least the Python argparse option parsing library has trouble parsing option values that may start with dashes (which can happen for tokens). So contrary to the syntax in the original report, no argument names are passed, only the options. Example:
`custom-provision-script simpleHttp .well-known/acme-challenge/token1 token1value example.com .well-known/acme-challenge/token2 token2value example.com`

This is enough to get me started using letsencrypt, but obviously lacks documentation and hasn't been polished, so I'm not proposing it for inclusion (⇒ no pull request). Feel free to use it as a basis for the official client, though.

> Am I right in saying that if the producer stopped producing, the consumer would have to consume consumer_lag + fetchq_cnt messages before it was completely caught up and had nothing more to do? Would fixing the bug you believe you have change that?

Yes. The code currently sort of assumes that the difference between the last fetched message and the last message handed to the application won't differ much, but with long processing time, or any Python code, that assumption might not be true.

I'm going to change the consumer_lag calculation from  'high_offset - last_fetched_offset' to 'high_offset - last_app_offset', i.e., the offset the latest message handed to the application - this is the true consumer lag.

> What's more, the numbers for fetchq_cnt seem to be the same across all 5 partitions always. I assume this is because there is only one fetchq, and its stats are simply replicated throughout the stats block?

Yes, with the high-level consumer (which the Python code always uses) all the fetchqs for all partitions are combined into one, so stats will report the same fetchq metrics for all partitions.


Until consumer_lag is fixed in librdkafka you should use `hi_offset - app_offset` to get the true consumer lag.

Exceptions are typically only thrown on invalid use of the APIs,

For recoverable runtime errors, such as ALL_BROKERS_DOWN, it would be counter-productive to raise an exception since the problem is most likely temporary and the client will recover automatically.

General errors are thus emitted to the error_cb, while producer delivery errors are emitted on the produce callback.

Inside `views.py`, `get_files_browse_urls` constructs the file context that is rendered in the browse view. `is_image` checks if the extension of a path is of an image. But with S3 `utils.get_media_url` will include GET parameters in the path, meaning `is_image` will return False. Either `is_image` should be called against `filename` variable or should chop off GET params.

> You mean the bit where streams would always PLAY_NOW instead of PLAY_ALL? Now that streams are just treated as regular tracks in the 'Playlists' pane I did not think that was necessary anymore.

Yes. But no, it's not to do with that. It's the case where you click a particular stream from a long list of results. Having all those streams added to your queue is almost never what you actually want to do.  And in the case of unreliable internet streams, if the one you clicked doesn't work then Mopidy will then spend time trying all the streams in turn until it finds one that works. For `append` calls, it's a little more complicated. On the one hand, `append`'s `Dispatcher` has a `dispatch` method that nicely returns the function that it will dispatch. So it's quite straightforward to see what will run and inspect/verify that call like I mentioned for `convert`. Unfortunately, many `append` functions are something like "append_anything_to_x" and just call `append` again with an object returned from `convert`. I'm not entirely sure what can be done about that situation. You could perhaps try patching `convert` and `append` with dummy functions that will return simulated results, but that doesn't seem safe to execute as the append function may run other things you don't want run in a simulation mode.

You could say that only getting back the function that is registered with `append` is good enough. For someone unit testing a new backend that's probably true and it may be sufficient to know that the function you registered gets called as expected - the testing of the internals of that function itself, including any embedded `append` or `convert` calls can be left to the person developing that backend. But this doesn't seem ideal for debugging or for situations where you need to confirm the path that a conversion will take. This may be another opportunity for test utilities that can help parse out the embedded convert or append calls and perhaps continue down the path. It's also very possible there's another more creative solution I'm not thinking of.

I've gotten more experience with test-driven development lately that has me wanting to pick this up again. odo seems a bit difficult to use in a TDD framework because it does quite a bit of magic behind the scenes.

One typical testing pattern when developing a new backend would be to create a function that does a conversion or append, testing it in isolation outside of odo. Then the integration with odo can be tested by registering that function, calling odo, and verifying that the newly registered function was called with the expected arguments, using a mock for the new function so you can inspect it and not have it run.

I don't think this approach will really work with odo though, because odo may do a whole lot more behind the scenes. It may call `resource` or `discover`, and the path from your source to target may go through an arbitrary number of other hops which you don't want actually called and shouldn't have to mock out.

Apologies this is a bit of a mess but unfortunately after getting my head around this, that post made sense. Which probably means I need to explain it in a meeting.

We basically have 1 loop running in the background managing Actions.
As well as this we have a thread-pool of 4 threads which all attempt to keep grabbing the next JobAction from the internal Que and then execute the function the JobAction wraps.

The machinery is such that the inifinite loop has break statements on shutdown and should send a message to all of the monitoring worker threads to stop at shutdown.
During running the threads keep trying to re-populate themselves by emptying the global Queue which is re-populated by the main monitoring loop.

I think both stacktraces are significant errors, actually.  The first one is from the Reloader thread trying to read directories off disk, and the second one is from the main server thread trying to launch the Werkzeug server.  These are both issues with trying to UTF-8 decode the hostname -
 first as written into the tfevents filename by TensorFlow, and second directly from the Python `socket` module methods.  On your particular Windows machine it looks like UTF-8 is not the actual encoding for the hostname and filenames.

It appears that the second issue with socket.gethostbyaddr() is a known Python bug involving Unicode hostnames on Windows, which looks it may be fixed in Python 3.5 per https://bugs.python.org/issue26227.  What python version are you using?  If it's less than 3.5, could you try again with 3.5?

The first issue might be something we'd have to fix within TensorFlow, though reproducing it may a little challenging for us.  Can you tell us the following:

1) exactly what version of Windows you're running
2) your active code page (you can see this by running the `chcp` command in cmd)
3) the result of running the following two python commands from the directory containing the "events.out.tfevents.1509071742.我的电脑" events file

```
python -c "import os; print(os.listdir(os.getcwdb()))"
```

(This one must be from an environment with tensorflow available, e.g. a virtualenv)
```
python -c "import os; import tensorflow as tf; tf.gfile.ListDirectory(os.getcwdb())"
```

I find the different views on how Openthread should evolve, very interesting.  I'm always surprised when I encounter views that differ so strongly with my own.  Human nature I guess.  In any case, I do feel strongly that Openthread should work very hard to limit its feature set to its core responsibilities; Thread networking.  If we abandon this tenet, it is my belief, we will find ourselves sliding down a slippery slope, forced to adopt an ever growing set of responsibilities that have little to do with moving packets on a Thread network. The larger the code base, the more maintenance effort is required and its this growing burden that I want to contain.

Previously we had a similar discussion about providing a flash API (read, write, erase you get the idea).  This API was in some cases needed for OT Settings storage (there is no rule that says settings must be stored in flash). In the end we agreed, that a pure flash API was more than OT should provide and a more direct otPlatSettings API was appropriate.  With that said, the flash API did not completely vanish.  It was repositioned in the examples folders and given a utils- prefix. In my mind, example code falls outside of the guaranteed source arena, meaning that the OT group is not required to maintain the examples code as strongly and diligently as the core.  It is here, in examples, that I believe we could add a hw timer api, if the desire is as strong as some here suggest. It could likewise be assigned the utils- prefix and implemented on an as needed basis for those platforms where it has use.

In this way the OT group provides source that can serve this need, but it is not required to do so for every platform that gets added to the supported set. Finally, bugs in the examples code base should naturally be assigned a lower priority over bugs in the core code.

> The intent of this feature was that a function returning a function doesn't need a return value policy (not a class_<> type), and that this flag could be forwarded to be used as the return value policy of the returned function so that the user has a way of customizing this behavior

As far as I understand, this forwarding behavior is exactly how it works now. The only issue is that
```c++
m.attr("func") = func; // implicit `py::cast()`
```
ends up behaving like
```c++
m.attr("func") = py::cpp_function(func, py::return_value_policy::copy);
```
instead of the expected
```c++
m.attr("func") = py::cpp_function(func); // implicit `py::return_value_policy::automatic`
```

The cause seems to be, as @henryiii pointed out, that `py::cast()` intercepts the policy and changes it to `copy` early on, so the `std::function` caster gets that instead of the original `automatic`.
