Basically you can think of this as being "if I put this cert up on a server on the Internet, is it going to work?" I think the main things we're checking here are:

* the cert file/chain hasn't been corrupted, or accidentally overwritten by the sys admin with different certs, or whatever
* the cert and chain are actually signed by a CA that's trusted by browsers (this should always be the case for LE's ACME endpoint, but maybe not if people run other ACME servers)
* datetime range. Currently we check on the expiry end of the range. But certs can also be "not yet valid". It may be that that only ever happens because the user is running Certbot on a machine with an incorrect clock. So in that case we probably want to print an error message saying "Probably your clock is wrong, or the ACME server has done something very weird".
* any other weird properties of certs that we haven't thought of but which might be important. There are theoretically a lot of those (see ef [Table V of this paper](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/~shmat/shmat_oak14.pdf), though again it would be very surprising if an ACME server ever issued a cert that failed such tests.

I thiiiink we already effectively check that the cert is in its datetime validity window, but implicitly we'll also be doing this

At least that was the reason I kept all task files until a project is completed. Although tasks are always inside steps, steps do not have to have signatures. An obvious example is for steps that do not specify output. In this case, the tasks will be run and re-run the steps will always trigger rerun of tasks. In the current implementation, tasks will be rerun in this case but signatures inside tasks might left them quit directly (though they will also be queued in PBS etc).

I do not know an all-around good solution, but at least we are at a point when we should decide on a reasonable solution.
This does not mean we cannot change it though.

> probably won't change anything if the ray start --block script exits ungracefully.

If you can't do this at the OS level (e.g. using prctl, having the child monitor the parent process (I usually open a pipe between them, have the child read in a loop, and exit when the pipe closes due to parent death), etc...), then one way to handle this cleanly is to use either [`weakref.finalize`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/weakref.html#weakref.finalize) or [atexit](https://docs.python.org/3/library/atexit.html) to register cleanup functions as you move through startup. Then cleanup will always (unless segfault or sigkill) be run on exit, even if an error/interrupt occurs midway through the process.

@bddppq Looks like using the Attr<AttributeProto::INT> way still can't resolve the compilation issue without cast. Below is what I tried, compiler still needs to have the cast for this call "Attr<AttributeProto::INT>("a", "desc", 1);". 

So, the last way to solve the problem is really doing renaming of each Attr API, which I personally think it looks a little bit ugly. I will go ahead with current solution then, of course will revisit this if you folks have better ideas (or you think renaming Attr APIs is a good idea). Thank you very much!

#define ATTR_SETTER_WITH_SINGLE_VALUE(type, field, attrtype)        \
    template<AttributeProto::AttributeType AttrType>                \
    OpSchema& Attr(const std::string& name,                         \
        const std::string& description,                             \
        const type& default_value);                                 \
                                                                    \
    template <>                                                     \
    OpSchema& Attr<attrtype>(const std::string& name,               \
        const std::string& description,                             \
        const type& default_value)                                  \
    {                                                               \
        AttributeProto a;                                           \
        a.set_name(name);                                           \
        a.set_##field(default_value);                               \
        a.set_type(attrtype);                                       \
        Attr(Attribute(name, description, a));                      \
        return *this;                                               \
    }                                                               \

    ATTR_SETTER_WITH_SINGLE_VALUE(int64_t, i, AttributeProto::INT);
    ATTR_SETTER_WITH_SINGLE_VALUE(float, f, AttributeProto::FLOAT);
